Use the search tool to find relevant information from the course materials before answering questions.

If you can find specific information through search, use it to provide accurate answers.

Always include references by citing the filename of the source material you used.
When citing the reference, replace "faq-main" by the full path to the GitHub repository: "https://github.com/DataTalksClub/faq/blob/main/"

If the search doesn't return relevant results, let the user know and provide general guidance.

parts.content: Is it okay to run the course on Windows, or should I switch to Linux or macOS for the shell scripts later in the curriculum?

parts.content: We need to answer based on course FAQ. Use search tool to find relevant info.

parts.args: {"query":"Windows Linux macOS shell scripts course run Windows okay"}

content.question: Environment: shell scripts (*.sh) don't work for Windows users without WSL
content.content: Several modules use shell scripts (`*.sh`) for setup or runtime tasks. Most Windows users running them outside WSL will hit issues — Git Bash and MINGW64 are not always sufficient. Set up a WSL environment from the start to avoid getting blocked partway through the course.

content.question: Which operating systems does the course support?
content.content:
Linux, macOS, and Windows all work. Students in the most recent cohorts have completed the course on all three. Linux is the smoothest by default.

On Windows, install WSL2 and run everything inside a WSL distro from the start. Git Bash and MINGW64 are not always sufficient for the shell scripts used in later modules.

content.question: Docker - error during connect: In the default daemon configuration on Windows, the docker client must be run with elevated privileges
content.content:
If you get this error:

```
docker: error during connect: In the default daemon configuration on Windows, the docker client must be run with elevated privileges to connect.: Post "http://%2F%2F.%2Fpipe%2Fdocker_engine/v1.24/containers/create": open //./pipe/docker_engine: The system cannot find the file specified.
See 'docker run --help'.
```

To resolve it on Windows, follow these guidelines based on your version:

**Windows 10 Pro / 11 Pro Users**:

* Ensure Hyper-V is enabled, as Docker can use it as a backend.
* Follow the [Enable Hyper-V Option on Windows 10 / 11](https://www.c-sharpcorner.com/article/install-and-configured-docker-desktop-in-windows-10/) tutorial.

**Windows 10 Home / 11 Home Users**:

* The 'Home' version doesn't support Hyper-V, so use WSL2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux).
* Refer to [install WSL on Windows 11](https://pureinfotech.com/install-wsl-windows-11/) for detailed instructions.

If you encounter the "WslRegisterDistribution failed with error: 0x800701bc" error:

- Update the WSL2 Linux Kernel by following the guidelines at [GitHub: WSL Issue 5393](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/5393).

content.question: Opening an HTML file with a Windows browser from Linux running on WSL
content.content:
If you’re running Linux on Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) 2, you can open HTML files from the guest (Linux) with any Internet Browser installed on the host (Windows). Just install [wslu](https://wslutiliti.es/wslu/install.html) and open the page using `wslview`:

```bash
wslview index.html
```

You can customize which browser to use by setting the `BROWSER` environment variable first. For example:

```bash
export BROWSER='/mnt/c/Program Files/Firefox/firefox.exe'
```

content.question: Docker: Should I run docker commands from the windows file system or a file system of a Linux distribution in WSL?
content.content:
If you're running a Home Edition, you can still make it work with WSL2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux) by following the [tutorial here](https://pureinfotech.com/install-wsl-windows-11/).

If even after making sure your WSL2 (or Hyper-V) is set up accordingly, Docker remains stuck, you can try the following options:

- [Reset to Factory Defaults](https://imgur.com/CfESyNt)
- Perform a fresh install.

parts.args: {"query":"Which operating systems does the course support?"}

content.question: Which operating systems does the course support?
content.content:
Linux, macOS, and Windows all work. Students in the most recent cohorts have completed the course on all three. Linux is the smoothest by default.

On Windows, install WSL2 and run everything inside a WSL distro from the start. Git Bash and MINGW64 are not always sufficient for the shell scripts used in later modules.

content.question: Course: When does the course start?
content.content:
A new cohort runs roughly January–April every year. For the current cohort's exact start date and registration link, check the [course repo README](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/data-engineering-zoomcamp).

- Register via the link in the course repo before the cohort starts.
- Join the [course Telegram channel](https://t.me/dezoomcamp) for announcements.
- Join DataTalks.Club's [Slack](https://datatalks.club/docs/general/slack/) and the `#course-data-engineering` channel.

content.question: Course: Can I get support if I take the course in the self-paced mode?
content.content:
Yes, the Slack channel remains open and you can ask questions there. However, always search the channel first and check the FAQ, as most likely your questions are already answered here.

You can also tag the bot `@ZoomcampQABot` to help you conduct the search, but don’t rely on its answers 100%.

content.question: GCP Free Trial vs Sandbox: which one should I use for the course?
content.content:
GCP has two free options. They are not equivalent for this course:

- Free Trial ($300 credit, 90 days). Required for the course — gives you VMs, GCS buckets, and full BigQuery functionality.
- Sandbox (free, no credit card). Limited services. It does not include VMs or GCS, and BigQuery features are restricted, so you cannot complete the course on Sandbox alone.

Use the Free Trial.

content.question: Course: What are the prerequisites for this course?
content.content:
To get the most out of this course, you should have:

- Basic coding experience
- Familiarity with SQL
- Experience with Python (helpful but not required)

No prior data engineering experience is necessary. See [Readme on GitHub](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/data-engineering-zoomcamp/blob/main/README.md#prerequisites).

If you have these basics, you're ready to start — you don't need to master everything up front. The course covers Git and GitHub (see *How do I use Git/GitHub for this course?*), and you'll pick up the command-line/Linux basics you need during the setup modules.

parts.content:
We have relevant info. Need to answer: Is it okay to run course on Windows, or should switch to Linux/macOS for shell scripts later? Use the info from the FAQ: Linux, macOS, Windows all work, but Windows needs WSL2 for shell scripts; Git Bash not sufficient. So answer: you can stay on Windows if you install WSL2 and run everything inside that; otherwise Linux/macOS smoother. Provide references.

Will cite both results: environment support question and environment roadblock for Windows users. Provide links with full GitHub path.

Let's construct answer.

You can absolutely take the Zoomcamp on a Windows machine — the course officially supports **Linux, macOS, and Windows**. However, many of the later modules rely on shell scripts (`*.sh`). Those scripts expect a genuine Unix‑like environment, so running them directly in a Windows console (Command Prompt, PowerShell, Git Bash, MINGW64, etc.) will often fail.

**What works best on Windows**

1. **Install WSL 2** (Windows Subsystem for Linux) and create a Linux distro (Ubuntu, Debian, etc.).  
2. Run **all of the course commands inside the WSL terminal**.  
3. Treat the WSL filesystem as your working directory – this gives you a true Bash environment and avoids the “shell‑script‑doesn’t‑run” roadblocks that many Windows users hit.

If you skip WSL and try to run the `.sh` files with Git Bash or MINGW64, you’ll likely encounter errors such as missing utilities or path‑translation problems. The FAQ notes that “Git Bash and MINGW64 are not always sufficient for the shell scripts used in later modules” and recommends using WSL from the start. [Environment: shell scripts (*.sh) don’t work for Windows users without WSL](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/faq/blob/main/faq-main/_questions/data-engineering-zoomcamp/environment/005_8b0214d089_environment-roadblock-for-windows-users-in-modules.md)

**If you prefer not to use WSL**

- Switching to a native Linux machine (or a macOS system) gives you the smoothest experience out of the box.  
- macOS already includes a Bash/Zsh terminal, so the scripts run without extra setup.

**Bottom line**

- **Windows + WSL 2** → fully supported, works for all modules.  
- **Linux/macOS** → works without extra steps and is the “smoothest by default”.  

So you don’t have to abandon Windows; just set up WSL 2 and run everything inside that Linux environment. [Which operating systems does the course support?](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/faq/blob/main/faq-main/_questions/data-engineering-zoomcamp/environment/019_a0e992e6ef_which-operating-systems-does-the-course-support.md)
